Optional list of B dicts for training loss computation.

members.summary: YOLOv1 single-shot object detector (Redmon et al., CVPR 2016).
members.extended:
The original "You Only Look Once" detector — recasts object detection
as a single regression problem.  An AlexNet-derived Darknet backbone
processes the whole image, then an adaptive-average-pool + two FC
layers produce an $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$ tensor:
for each of $S^2$ grid cells, $B$ candidate boxes
$(x, y, w, h, \mathrm{conf})$ and $C$ class probabilities.
A box is responsible for an object iff the object's centre falls
inside that cell.  No anchors, no region proposals — a single
forward pass yields all detections at 45 fps on a Titan X.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `yolo_v1` for the full
Darknet backbone or `yolo_v1_tiny` for the lightweight
Tiny variant; both default to PASCAL VOC's `S = 7`,
`B = 2`, `C = 20`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v1 import yolo_v1
>>> model = yolo_v1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 448, 448)   # YOLOv1 trained at 448
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[-1] >= 20
True

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: darknet
attributes.description:
Backbone convolutional stack (full or tiny depending on
`config.tiny`).

attributes.name: pool
attributes.description: Pools backbone output to a fixed $S \times S$ map.

attributes.name: fc1, fc2
attributes.description:
Two-layer FC head producing the
$S \cdot S \cdot (B \cdot 5 + C)$ flat prediction tensor.

members.name: yolo_v1
members.summary: YOLOv1 with full Darknet backbone (Redmon et al., CVPR 2016).
members.extended:
Builds the paper-cited full YOLOv1 detector: 24-layer Darknet
backbone, 7x7 grid, 2 boxes per cell, 20 PASCAL VOC classes (or
80 COCO classes if overridden).  Approximately 270M parameters
(most in the two 4096-dim FC layers) and 63.4% mAP on VOC 2007
test (paper Table 1) at 45 fps.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV1Config` —
`num_classes`, `split_size` (S), `num_boxes` (B),
`score_thresh`, `nms_thresh`, and loss weights.

returns.description:
Detector with the full YOLOv1 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v1 import yolo_v1
>>> model = yolo_v1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 448, 448)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon et al., "You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object
Detection", CVPR 2016 ([arXiv:1506.02640](https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.02640)).

members.name: yolo_v1_tiny
members.summary: YOLOv1-Tiny with reduced Darknet backbone (Redmon et al., CVPR 2016).
members.extended:
Builds the lightweight variant explicitly described in the YOLOv1
paper alongside the full model: 9-layer Darknet-Tiny backbone with
the same 7x7 grid and 2 boxes per cell.  Approximately 45M
parameters and 52.7% mAP on VOC 2007 at 155 fps (paper Table 1) —
trades accuracy for speed.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV1Config`.

returns.description:
Detector with the YOLOv1-Tiny configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v1 import yolo_v1_tiny
>>> model = yolo_v1_tiny()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 448, 448)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon et al., "You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object
Detection", CVPR 2016 ([arXiv:1506.02640](https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.02640)).  The tiny variant uses
fewer / shallower conv stages but keeps the same FC head topology.

members.summary: YOLOv2 / YOLO9000 object detector (Redmon & Farhadi, CVPR 2017).
members.extended:
The successor to YOLOv1 with several decisive upgrades: a deeper
**Darknet-19** backbone with batch normalisation, **anchor boxes**
(5 k-means-clustered priors on COCO instead of YOLOv1's
direct-regression "anchor-free" approach), and a **passthrough**
layer that injects fine-grained stride-16 features into the
stride-32 detection head via space-to-depth reorganisation.
Anchors give per-cell predictions an inductive bias on shape that
YOLOv1 lacks, and the passthrough recovers spatial detail lost in
the final downsample — together they bump VOC 2007 mAP from 63.4%
(YOLOv1) to 78.6% (paper Table 4).

The output tensor $(B, A \cdot (5 + C), fH, fW)$ encodes
$A$ anchors per cell with $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h, \mathrm{conf})$ deltas plus $C$ class scores.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `yolo_v2` or
`yolo_v2_tiny` for the standard variants.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v2 import yolo_v2
>>> model = yolo_v2()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 416, 416)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon & Farhadi, "YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger",
CVPR 2017 ([arXiv:1612.08242](https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.08242)).  Per-anchor box decoding uses the
"direct location prediction" parameterisation:



$$
\begin{aligned}
    b_x &= \sigma(t_x) + c_x, & b_w &= p_w \exp(t_w), \\
    b_y &= \sigma(t_y) + c_y, & b_h &= p_h \exp(t_h),
\end{aligned}
$$

where $(c_x, c_y)$ is the cell top-left in feature coords and
$(p_w, p_h)$ is the anchor's prior size.  The
sigmoid-on-$t_{x,y}$ bounds the centre to lie inside the
responsible cell — a stability win over YOLOv1's unbounded
regression.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: backbone
attributes.description:
Darknet-19 trunk producing a 512-channel stride-16 route feature
and a 1024-channel stride-32 trunk feature.

attributes.name: det1, det2, det3
attributes.description:
Stride-32 head convolutions; `det3` consumes the concatenation
of the 2048-channel space-to-depth passthrough and the 1024-channel
`det2` output.

attributes.name: pred
attributes.description:
Final 1x1 conv producing $A \cdot (5 + C)$ channels (no
BatchNorm, no activation).

members.name: yolo_v2
members.summary: YOLOv2 / YOLO9000 with Darknet-19 backbone (Redmon & Farhadi, CVPR 2017).
members.extended:
Builds the paper-cited YOLOv2 detector: 19-layer Darknet-19 backbone,
5 anchor priors clustered by k-means on the training set, passthrough
space-to-depth layer, and 80 COCO classes by default.  At the
standard 416x416 input the output grid is 13x13 (stride 32).
Approximately 50M parameters; COCO test-dev mAP@.5 of 44.0%
(paper Table 4).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV2Config` —
`num_classes`, `num_anchors`, anchor priors, `score_thresh`,
`nms_thresh`, etc.

returns.description:
Detector with the standard YOLOv2 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v2 import yolo_v2
>>> model = yolo_v2()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 416, 416)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon & Farhadi, "YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger",
CVPR 2017 ([arXiv:1612.08242](https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.08242)).  The five default anchors come from
k-means clustering of VOC / COCO ground-truth boxes (paper Table 1).

members.name: yolo_v2_tiny
members.summary: YOLOv2-Tiny — lightweight Darknet variant of YOLOv2.
members.extended:
Builds a compact YOLOv2 detector with a slimmer Darknet backbone
(no bottleneck stages); same anchor priors and detection head shape
as `yolo_v2`.  Used as the speed-optimised reference variant
in the YOLO9000 release notes.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV2Config`.

returns.description:
Detector with the YOLOv2-Tiny configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v2 import yolo_v2_tiny
>>> model = yolo_v2_tiny()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 416, 416)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
Lighter backbone alternative to `yolo_v2`; head topology is
unchanged.

members.extended:
YOLOv3 (Redmon & Farhadi, 2018) uses a Darknet-53 backbone with three
detection scales at strides 8, 16, and 32 (P3/P4/P5).  Each scale has 3
hand-picked COCO anchors for small, medium, and large objects respectively.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description: Number of foreground classes (COCO default = 80).

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Input image channels.

parameters.name: anchors
parameters.description:
9 (width, height) anchor pairs in pixels, 3 per scale.
           Order: P3 (small), P4 (medium), P5 (large).

parameters.name: strides
parameters.description: Feature-map strides for P3, P4, P5.

parameters.name: score_thresh
parameters.description: Minimum objectness × class score to keep a detection.

parameters.name: nms_thresh
parameters.description: IoU threshold for per-class NMS.

parameters.name: lambda_coord
parameters.description: Box regression loss weight.

parameters.name: lambda_noobj
parameters.description: Objectness loss weight for negative anchors.

members.name: YOLOV3ForObjectDetection

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, targets: list[dict[str, Tensor]] | None = None)

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: (B, C, H, W) image batch.

parameters.name: targets
parameters.description: Optional list of target dicts per image.

returns.description:
`ObjectDetectionOutput`:
`logits`    : (B, total_anchors, C) raw class logits.
`pred_boxes`: (B, total_anchors, 4) xyxy decoded boxes.
`loss`      : loss scalar when targets provided.

methods.name: postprocess
methods.summary: Filter by score, clip boxes, apply per-class NMS.

parameters.name: output
parameters.description: Forward pass output.

parameters.name: image_sizes
parameters.description: List of (H, W) per image.

members.extended:
Adds a 53-layer **Darknet-53** backbone (residual connections, more
capacity than Darknet-19) and an FPN-style multi-scale detection
head that predicts on three feature levels — $P_3$ (stride 8),
$P_4$ (stride 16), $P_5$ (stride 32) — with three
anchors per cell per level (9 anchors total).  This multi-scale
detection dramatically improves small-object recall over YOLOv2,
bringing COCO AP up to 33.0% (paper Table 3).  Per-class predictions
use independent sigmoids rather than softmax — supporting
multi-label classification.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `yolo_v3` for the full
Darknet-53 model or `yolo_v3_tiny` for the lightweight
2-scale variant.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v3 import yolo_v3
>>> model = yolo_v3()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 416, 416)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon & Farhadi, "YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement", 2018
([arXiv:1804.02767](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.02767)).  Per-anchor box decoding reuses the YOLOv2
direct-location parameterisation; the multi-class objective swaps
YOLOv2's softmax for per-class binary cross-entropy:



$$
\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{cls} = -\sum_{c = 1}^{C}
    \bigl[\,y_c \log \sigma(s_c) + (1 - y_c) \log(1 - \sigma(s_c))\bigr],
$$

enabling multi-label predictions where a single object can belong
to multiple categories (e.g. "Woman" + "Person").

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: backbone
attributes.description:
Residual backbone producing P3 / P4 / P5 features at strides
8 / 16 / 32 (128 / 256 / 1024 channels).

attributes.name: p5_compress, p5_predict
attributes.description:
Stride-32 detection head: five-conv compress + 3x3 + 1x1
prediction conv producing $3(5 + C)$ channels.

attributes.name: p4_compress, p4_predict, p4_to_p3_conv
attributes.description:
Stride-16 head with FPN-style top-down upsample + concatenation
with the P4 backbone feature.

attributes.name: p3_compress, p3_predict, p5_to_p4_conv
attributes.description:
Stride-8 head with another top-down upsample + concatenation
with the P3 backbone feature.

members.name: yolo_v3
members.summary: YOLOv3 with Darknet-53 backbone, 3-scale detection (Redmon & Farhadi, 2018).
members.extended:
Builds the paper-cited YOLOv3 detector: 53-layer residual Darknet
backbone + FPN-style top-down head producing predictions at
strides 8 / 16 / 32 with 3 anchors per cell per scale (9 anchors
total, COCO k-means clustered).  Default 80 COCO classes; reaches
COCO test-dev AP of 33.0% at 416x416 input (paper Table 3).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, attempt to load pretrained COCO weights.  Currently
raises `NotImplementedError`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV3Config`.

returns.description:
Detector with the standard YOLOv3 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v3 import yolo_v3
>>> model = yolo_v3()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 416, 416)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon & Farhadi, "YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement", 2018
([arXiv:1804.02767](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.02767)).  The multi-scale head is the principal upgrade
over YOLOv2 and the source of its small-object accuracy gain.

members.name: yolo_v3_tiny
members.extended:
Builds the YOLOv3-Tiny detector explicitly defined in the paper's
accompanying release: shallower Darknet-Tiny backbone with only two
detection scales (strides 16 and 32) and 3 anchors per scale.
Approximately 8.7M parameters; targets edge / real-time deployment.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, attempt to load pretrained COCO weights.  Currently
raises `NotImplementedError`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV3Config`.

returns.description:
Detector with the YOLOv3-Tiny configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v3 import yolo_v3_tiny
>>> model = yolo_v3_tiny()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 416, 416)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Redmon & Farhadi, "YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement", 2018
([arXiv:1804.02767](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.02767)).  Drops one detection scale and uses a much
smaller backbone — the standard "tiny" variant ships alongside the
full release for embedded inference.

SPP + PANet as neck, and three detection scales at strides 8/16/32.

          Order: P3 (small), P4 (medium), P5 (large).

members.summary: YOLOv4 multi-scale object detector (Bochkovskiy et al., 2020).
members.extended:
A heavily engineered iteration over YOLOv3 that combines several
independently-published improvements ("bag of freebies" and "bag
of specials" in the paper's terminology) into a single
high-throughput detector.  The core architectural changes are:

- **CSPDarknet-53** backbone — replaces residual blocks with
  Cross-Stage-Partial blocks that split and re-merge the feature
  stream, reducing FLOPs without hurting accuracy.
- **SPP** (Spatial Pyramid Pooling) module on the final backbone
  stage — fuses three max-pooled receptive fields plus the identity,
  widening the effective receptive field.
- **PANet** (Path Aggregation Network) neck — adds a bottom-up path
  on top of the FPN top-down path, giving each prediction level
  access to both fine and coarse features.

Heads remain YOLOv3-style (three scales, 3 anchors / scale), but
training switches the box-regression loss to **CIoU** which couples
centre distance, IoU, and aspect-ratio consistency in a single
differentiable objective.  COCO test-dev AP of 43.5% at 65 fps on a
Tesla V100 (paper Table 8).

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `yolo_v4` for the standard
full-size model.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v4 import yolo_v4
>>> model = yolo_v4()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 608, 608)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Bochkovskiy et al., "YOLOv4: Optimal Speed and Accuracy of
Object Detection", arXiv 2020 ([arXiv:2004.10934](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.10934)).  Complete-IoU
(CIoU) loss is defined as



$$
\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{CIoU} =
    1 - \mathrm{IoU} + \frac{\rho^2(b, b^\mathrm{gt})}{c^2}
    + \alpha v,
\qquad
v = \frac{4}{\pi^2}
    \Bigl(\arctan\frac{w^\mathrm{gt}}{h^\mathrm{gt}} -
          \arctan\frac{w}{h}\Bigr)^2,
$$

where $\rho$ is the Euclidean distance between box centres,
$c$ is the diagonal of the smallest enclosing box, and
$\alpha$ is a balancing trade-off term.  CIoU converges faster
than IoU / GIoU and is the standard regression objective from
YOLOv4 onwards.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: backbone
attributes.description: Cross-Stage-Partial Darknet-53 producing P3 / P4 / P5 features.

attributes.name: neck
attributes.description:
SPP + PANet (top-down FPN + bottom-up path) producing the three
head input features.

attributes.name: p3_head, p4_head, p5_head
attributes.description:
Three-scale prediction heads, each producing $3 (5 + C)$
channels.

members.name: yolo_v4
members.summary: YOLOv4 — CSPDarknet-53 + SPP + PANet (Bochkovskiy et al., 2020).
members.extended:
Builds the paper-cited full-size YOLOv4 detector: CSPDarknet-53
backbone, SPP module on the final stage, PANet (top-down +
bottom-up) neck, and 3-scale detection at strides 8 / 16 / 32 with
3 anchors / scale.  Default 80 COCO classes; reaches COCO test-dev
AP of 43.5% at 65 fps on Tesla V100 (paper Table 8).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, attempt to load pretrained COCO weights.  Currently
raises `NotImplementedError`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `YOLOV4Config`.

returns.description:
Detector with the standard YOLOv4 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.yolo._v4 import yolo_v4
>>> model = yolo_v4()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 608, 608)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape[0]
1
notes:
See Bochkovskiy et al., "YOLOv4: Optimal Speed and Accuracy of
Object Detection", arXiv 2020 ([arXiv:2004.10934](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.10934)).  The paper's
"bag of specials" includes Mish activation, CIoU loss, DropBlock
regularisation, Mosaic augmentation, and CmBN — all motivated by
independent prior work that YOLOv4 aggregates and tunes together.

summary: YOLO family — You Only Look Once object detectors.
notes:
- YOLOv1: Redmon et al., CVPR 2016
- YOLOv2: Redmon & Farhadi, CVPR 2017 (YOLO9000)
- YOLOv3: Redmon & Farhadi, arXiv 2018
- YOLOv4: Bochkovskiy et al., arXiv 2020
citation: Redmon, Joseph, et al. "You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection." Proceedings of the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2016, pp. 779–788.
theory:
YOLOv1 reframes detection as a **single regression problem**.
The input image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid; each
cell is responsible for objects whose centre falls inside it.
For each cell the network predicts $B$ candidate boxes
$(x, y, w, h, \mathrm{conf})$ plus one shared class
distribution over $C$ categories, so the head output has
shape $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$.

A single CNN (the Darknet trunk in the paper) processes the full
image once and emits all detections in one forward pass.  The
confidence target is $\Pr(\mathrm{obj}) \cdot \mathrm{IoU}^{\mathrm{truth}}_{\mathrm{pred}}$ so that confidence
encodes both objectness and localisation quality.  Training uses
a weighted sum-squared-error loss



$$
\mathcal{L} =
    \lambda_{\mathrm{coord}} \sum_{i,j}
      \mathbb{1}^{\mathrm{obj}}_{ij}
      \bigl[(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\bigr]
  + \lambda_{\mathrm{coord}} \sum_{i,j}
      \mathbb{1}^{\mathrm{obj}}_{ij}
      \bigl[(\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2
          + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2\bigr]
  + \sum_{i,j} \mathbb{1}^{\mathrm{obj}}_{ij}
      (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2
  + \lambda_{\mathrm{noobj}} \sum_{i,j}
      \mathbb{1}^{\mathrm{noobj}}_{ij}
      (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2
  + \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}^{\mathrm{obj}}_{i}
      \sum_{c}(p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2,
$$

where $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ make the regression less
sensitive to absolute scale and $\lambda_{\mathrm{coord}}, \lambda_{\mathrm{noobj}}$ rebalance the dense background term.

Because the entire pipeline is a single CNN, YOLOv1 runs at
45 fps (155 fps for the *tiny* variant) — orders of magnitude
faster than the two-stage R-CNN family — establishing the
one-stage detection paradigm.  Trade-off: each cell predicts a
fixed small $B$, limiting recall on clustered small
objects, which is exactly what YOLOv2/v3/v4 progressively fix
with anchors, multi-scale heads, and a stronger backbone/neck.
